sections.title: Overview
sections.content: High-quality chest compressions are the cornerstone of resuscitation. They generate approximately 25–30% of normal cardiac output and maintain cerebral and myocardial perfusion during cardiac arrest. Minimising interruptions to compressions is one of the most important modifiable factors in survival.

sections.title: Adult Technique
sections.content:
**Position:**

1. Patient supine on firm surface

2. Kneel beside patient at chest level

3. Place heel of hand on lower half of sternum (centre of chest)

4. Place second hand on top; interlace fingers; keep fingers off chest

**Compressions:**

- Depth: 5–6 cm
- Rate: 100–120/min
- Full chest recoil between compressions - do not lean
- Compression:ventilation ratio: 30:2 (BLS); continuous compressions with 1 breath/6 sec (advanced airway)
- Minimise interruptions - pause < 5 seconds for any intervention

sections.title: Paediatric Technique
sections.content:
**Infant (< 1 year):**
- Two-finger technique (single rescuer) or two-thumb encircling technique (two rescuers)
- Depth: 4 cm (approximately ⅓ AP diameter)
- Rate: 100–120/min
- Ratio: 15:2 (two rescuers) or 30:2 (single rescuer)

**Child (1 year to puberty):**
- One or two-hand technique depending on size
- Depth: 5 cm (approximately ⅓ AP diameter)
- Rate: 100–120/min
- Ratio: 15:2 (two rescuers)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Rotate compressor every 2 minutes to maintain quality
- Use feedback device if available (metronome, CPR prompt)
- Do not stop compressions to check rhythm unless defibrillator is charged and ready
- Consider mechanical CPR device for prolonged resuscitation or during transport
- CPR fraction > 80% is associated with improved survival
